comments.title: Dolmen Arch Healing Work Modifications
comments.content:
JMG,

I'm planning on performing the Sphere of Protection for my girlfriend, along with the "secret work" version of the healing exercise. (She's requested it.) I'd like to run my plan by you and see if it all checks out.

I'll be doing all of the same work on myself a few hours beforehand. This will be the complete "secret work of the grail" (healing version). Then I will perform it on/for my girlfriend. All of this is fairly straightforward as outlined in the final grade of the Dolmen Arch.

However, since she wants the SoP performed for her, I'm planning some modifications. My plan is in two parts:

A) to start her session by performing the standard SoP around her, using the boilerplate intention but specifying her as the recipient, and visualising all of the usual effects of the seven gates happening to her instead of me.

Then, B) I'll do the Three Cauldrons, Rising Dragons, and Tree of Light on myself as usual, open my palm centers, perform the Inner Grail Working with a healing intention for her, perform the healing passes on her, and perform the Circulation of Light centered on her.

Part B is the standard procedure from the lesson packet. What I'm curious about is part A, and the transition from part A to part B. Specifically:

1) My girlfriend has no experience with magic, and no intention of picking it up, but is interested and very supportive of my work. Is it okay to perform the SoP on someone as I've outlined? Is there anything I should do differently?

2) As written in the final grade's lesson packet, when performing the healing work on another the preliminary SoP would still be done on myself. Is it alright to do as I'm planning and perform the SoP on her, then the inner alchemical work on myself, then move on to healing? As I mentioned, I'll have done the full standard sequence on myself a few hours earlier.


Thanks in advance!

P.S. I've done the basic healing work on her a few times already, but have not yet done the more advanced version from the final grade for her, if that's relevant.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Healing Work Modifications
replies.content:
1) It is indeed okay to use the SoP that way, and the pattern of work you've sketched out is entirely appropriate.

2) If you've done the full sequence on yourself earlier in the same day, you should be fine going ahead as described.

I'm delighted to see this, btw -- original work with the Dolmen Arch system. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Healing Work Modifications
replies.content:
Thank you!

Regarding the original work, I've quite a few things I'd like to try once I've finished the course. I even have a document of notes on my computer titled "Expansion and Innovation in the Dolmen Arch Tradition" I'm considering taking some time after the DA to learn a different system, and then coming back to do the "innovation and expansion," or re-doing the whole Dolmen Arch over again, but I haven't made up my mind yet. I'm definitely planning on bending your ear throughout the process, though!

Thank you so much for putting the Dolmen Arch out there, and for all of the tutoring you've given me. The Dolmen Arch has been my spiritual home. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Healing Work Modifications
replies.content: You're most welcome -- and thank you for taking it so seriously.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I have no idea. I'm not into those, and most trad occultists tended to disapprove of mixing magic and psychedelics, so you're in largely unexplored territory.

2) Relentless practice. You're in one of the standard stages of the process, where you've learned the practices but haven't yet developed the inner capacities and skills that will allow you to perceive what you're doing. One book describes it as being set to polish a piece of jade that you can neither see nor feel! It's awkward, and can be very frustrating, but there's only one way to get to the point of being able to perceive what you're doing, and that's unremitting daily practice. Don't worry, you'll get there.

replies.content:
Regarding psychoactive substances, I would draw a comparison with a race car using nitrous oxide. Unless you are in some highly specialized class or have plenty of money to keep swapping out blown engines then it is likely better to focus on fundamentals. Neglecting survival techniques and relying on the NO to compensate for inexperience will just make you hit the wall that much faster.

As far as recreational I have come to the conclusion that moderation is key, because too much actually makes it more difficult for me to concentrate when it wears off, but your mileage may vary.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Neglecting survival techniques and relying on the NO to compensate for inexperience will just make you hit the wall that much faster."

This is a perfect analogy.

In my experience, psychedelics seem to increase sensitivity at the cost of agency. This can be okay if you're watching a movie or listening to an album (it can even turn the entertainment into an initiatory experience), but is usually a hindrance for anything requiring your active attention.

That being said, everyone's body responds differently, so see what works for your system.

When I first started "dabbling" in occultism, I thought it got me into a more spiritual frame of mind and made me more open to mystical experiences. When I began a daily practice in the Golden Dawn tradition as taught by JMG, I found that even the smallest amount of cannabis made me extremely uncomfortable (it remained mystical and perhaps even spiritual, but more along the lines of a bad trip). Another data point that may be of interest, I have several neopagan associates who use psychedelics and regard them as important to their magic rituals; I would be very reluctant to use them in a magical context at this point, personally.

comments.title: Hellenic SoP
comments.content:
I have just started going through your multi-part post on the Sphere of Protection. It's past time I took up a regular banishing ritual and did it right. I'm hoping for a little guidance on which specific divine names from the Greek pantheon would be best for each part. My personal patron is Athena.
Thank you for all you do.

JH

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content:
The Greek pantheon gives you a lot of options here. One of them runs like this:

East: Athena
West: Aphrodite
South: Apollo
North: Dionysos
Below: Demeter
Above: Zeus

If you have trouble with any of those deities ask me and I can suggest replacements.

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: No problems at all with any of them. Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: Could you make a similar suggestion for Heathens? I call on Odin, Frygg, and Baldur at the opening, but have trouble associating the rest of the Aesir with any particular elements beyond Hel for spirit below - and that’s not entirely clear. I know we call it the underworld, but it’s not necessarily located ‘under’.

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content:
Don't be too rigid about the elemental attributions, and remember that deities can be in both the opening and the calling of the elements. One arrangement that comes quite readily to mind:

East: Thor
West: Frey
South: Freya
North: Frigg
Below: Jörð
Above: Odin

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: That’s helpful. Thanks for that.

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: If we examine the placement of those gods and goddesses in 3D, gods form an upward triangle and goddesses form a downward triangle. Is that configuration relevant to polar/sexual attributions of these triangles?

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: You could certainly take it that way.

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: I know there are different versions of the SoP, but I had understood that I should choose gods related to the elements as well as the direction. So I have been using East (Air) Aether, South (Fire) Hephaestus, West (water) Poseidon and North (earth) Demeter, with moderate effect. Can that be improved upon? What would the invocation be in the case of, say, one of the alternatives you give? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Hellenic SoP
replies.content: The elemental attributions have plenty of wiggle room. Thus, in the example above, I used Athena for east/air as a goddess of intellect, Aphrodite for west/water as a goddess of emotion who rose from the sea, Apollo for fire/south because he's a sun god and the sun is at its height in the south, Dionysos for earth/north because he polarizes well with Apollo and because he traditionally came from the north, Demeter below because she's the goddess of agricultural fertility and all that rises from underground, and Zeus above because he's the storm and lightning god. As for the invocations, I'd use the same ones, making only such modifications as seem helpful.

comments.title: Considering options
comments.content:
I am considering which to spend my limited time working on: The Dolmen Arch or The Mysteries of Merlin. Could you please give a snapshot of the focus and intended outcome of each? Obviously the Mysteries are seasonal rituals to be worked while the other is daily study and practice, but I'm asking more about the changes intended to be provoked in the practitioner. Are they fairly similar, or are there important differences to consider?
-Korellyn

replies.title: Re: Considering options
replies.content:
There are huge differences between these two books. The Mysteries of Merlin is a set of eight seasonal rituals meant to accelerate the spiritual development of the practitioner in a relatively gentle way; it can be combined with many other sets of magical training, though it's closest to the material in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn (with which it shares extensive ritual and philosophical connections). Its goal is to awaken wisdom and to increase the practitioner's ability to be aware of, and to attune to, spiritual forces.

The Dolmen Arch is a complete curriculum of occult training. It's not merely daily practice, it's a whole series of practices, studies, disciplines, exercises, rituals, and meditations, not to mention a whale of a lot of philosophy and symbolism. It's intended to make the student a fully trained occultist capable of doing a wide range of different modes of internal work, including healing, enchantment, and initiation -- and the goal of training is among other things teaching people to teach and pass on the system.

Does that help at all?

replies.title: Re: Considering options
replies.content: Yes it does, thank you. Now I need to ponder for a bit.

replies.title: Re: Considering options
replies.content: This sounds interesting! Is Mysteries compatible with the LRM system?

replies.title: Re: Considering options
replies.content: With modifications, yes, but you'll need to go on to Circles of Power or some other more detailed textbook of Golden Dawn magic to adapt the Octagram Ritual (one of the core rituals in The Mysteries of Merlin to GD usage.

replies.content: Oh, here you answered my question...

comments.title: Water
comments.content:
I don’t know if this is magic, or occultism, but I figured I’d share my story.

I live on a mountain in Japan right near a river. Everything above me is pure forest and mountain. There’s a place by the river where water comes out of a little spout. Don’t know why it’s there, because there’s a sign that says, “Don’t drink this water. Contains e.Coli”. I’ve seen people come to collect the water in big jugs. They have said they boil it before using.

A few weeks ago, however, I saw somebody drinking right from the tap. Is it OK? I ask. Sure! he says. So I try it. Dang, it tastes awesome. I’ve always been of the “H2O is H2O camp,” but there is something special about this water.

At first it just tasted really good, but then I began to feel like it was actively nourishing and fulfilling me in some really subtle way. Now I drink it almost exclusively. When I have to drink other water, it feels literally dead, whereas my river water is alive.

Last week I hiked deep into that mountain. Came across the river, far upstream. Said thank you to the river spirit. Made friends. Felt like I gained an ally. :)

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content: I'm in the process of rereading Carmen Blacker's fine book The Catalpa Bow, which is a study of Japanese folk magic and religion, and your story sounds like something straight out of her pages. Clearly you and the river kami have established a good relationship. Have you considered performing misogi shuho in the river? You might find it an extraordinary experience.

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content:
Not sure what that is, but I’ll look it up.

It’s funny, I still consider myself on the highly skeptical side, and maybe it’s that I’ve lived in Japan for decades, but the idea that there are various spirits animating certain things just seems like common sense to me at this point.

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content: There's a good description of misogi shuho here -- the website belongs to one of the few Shinto shrines in North America (I wish there were more), and I've practiced misogi shuho with the chief priest of the shrine.

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content: Can you tell us about it?

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content:
Sure. Imagine yourself wearing nothing but a skimpy little Japanese loincloth and a headband with sacred characters on it, walking down to the bank of the Pilchuck River beind a muscular martial artist, similarly attired. The river in question has its source on a mountain called Glacier Peak, so I'll let you guess the water temperature! You stand on the bank while the martial artist guy, who is also a Shinto priest, does certain ceremonies, and then in you go, up to your neck in frigid water, with your hands folded in a particular way, chanting an incantation in archaic Japanese. It's stunningly cold...

And then the inner fire kicks in, hard. You have no trouble remaining there and wading back up out of the river when the ritual's finished. You head back into the shrine buildings, towel off, get dressed, and enjoy a cup of tea while talking shop with the priest.

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content:
hungryghost108,

I also live in Japan (though it's a big place so who knows how far away from you), and I have some experience with at least some of the contents of your conversation with JMG. I've sent you a personal message via Dreamwidth; if you're interested, please feel free to get in touch.

replies.title: Re: Water (and E Coli)
replies.content:
Here's what

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6262807/

says about E Coli:

"Most Escherichia coli strains in the human intestine are harmless. However, enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC) is a foodborne pathogen that causes intestinal disease in humans. "

So, whoever tested the water probably had no reason to distinguish between pathogenic and non-pathogenic strains, and couldn't assure visitors that the strain(s) in the water might not change. "Enterohemorrhagic" sounds like a bad symptom to me, so consider the risk that you're taking. That's why there's a sign. (In the US, they'd probably build something to make it impossible to fill a jug there. Too many US citizens are too functionally illiterate to heed a warning sign.)

Lathechuck

replies.title: Re: Water
replies.content: Stephen Harrod Buhner has written a lot about "wild water" and how much better it is to drink than tap or distilled water, it's more alive.

comments.title: Broken arm; Broken practice?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I’ve been a bummer magnet since February. Mom had a stroke, brother died, and today I got stupid in the garden while pruning and broke my arm. A few questions, please:

1) Does karmic culmination get metered out over time? No such thing as one and done? Intermittent waves of the stuff?
2) I’ve practiced all of the three Heathen rituals you posted daily since pretty much the day you posted each one. It may be a few days before I can work those out left handed and manage to stand without jarring my broken arm. Will this pause mess me over?
3) Is there a way to offer up this misfortune to the greater good? Seems like this was discussed here not long ago.

Thank you for your generosity.
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Broken arm; Broken practice?
replies.content:
1) It really varies depending on how much unresolved business you have. Unless you're pretty clean, "one and done" could get pretty horrific.

2) No, not at all. You might try, though, doing them in imagination. Sit comfortably, and imagine yourself rising from the chair and performing the rituals. Make it as vivid as you can. That's excellent training, and nearly as effective as doing it in your physical body.

3) Yes. Each night, before you go to sleep, review what you've suffered that day and say, silently or oout loud, something on the order of "May these misfortunes help clear away some of the karmic burdened of this troubled age."

replies.title: Re: Broken arm; Broken practice?
replies.content: By offering it up to the greater good, does that mean that one does not actually then get to clear their own karma via that misfortune?

replies.title: Re: Broken arm; Broken practice?
replies.content: It's complex. Yes, that means that the specific karmic pattern that brought on that misfortune is not cleared, but that very act, through its unselfishness, generates a great deal of positive karma. If you can actually, wholeheartedly dedicate your own suffering to clearing away collective negative karma, that will generate so much positive karma that it will overwhelm your personal negative karma. That's one of the reasons that mystics subject themselves to austerities -- by accepting physical discomfort and the like and dedicating that experience to expiating collective karma, they help everyone and also put their own spiritual development into overdrive.

comments.title: An assorted set of questions
comments.content:
1) There are many myths of creation and many of those talk about how human beings where created by the gods. A while ago you mentioned how we are pushed into evolving into more elevated beings by creation. What is it that was created by the gods, the infrastructure that allows for our souls to evolve —our body, the material world — or is it ourselves per se?

2) On a MM I think I recall you said you’ve worked a lot with meme magic. What sort of things does meme magic involve and what can be accomplished with it?

3)On self-unfoldment by disinclines of self realization Hall says that all spiritual practices should be dropped while taking the course. Did you find that necessary with your magical practices for example when you went through it?

4)It was mentioned that praying to the Christian god could have the effects of having strong desires to get more involved with it, for example wanting to get baptized etc. Does the GD with its Judeo Christian symbolism have the same effect?

4a) This got me thinking into the nature of what we want for ourselves and on the basis that we take decisions. Also, with meditation it has become increasingly clear which thoughts are mine, which thoughts I gathered for somewhere else. This triggered some sort of panic because I became aware that we kind of have very limited control on the myriad of influences that we have. Some are contextual from birth and some come from the society that we live in. But if all of these are just accumulations where does our free-will land in all of this if the desires and wants that we have come from some limited context?

5) When we step out of incarnation. We shed the material body, then the etheric double in stages. Does this happen all the way up to spirit? If so, is our next set of bodies influenced by the previous somehow?

replies.title: Re: An assorted set of questions
replies.content:
1) All of the above.

2) I should start by saying that I mean "memes" in Dawkins' original sense, not in the restricted internet sense of an image with some Lolcat prose on it. A meme is a specific self-replicating idea. A meme in that sense is a very effective way to shape public discussion and collective consciousness.

3) To my mind Hall was being overcautious. I simply used that practice as my method of meditation during the year I devoted to it, and maintained other practices -- notably the Sphere of Protection and daily divination.

4) No, it has its own egregor and tends to draw people into that, not into the church.

4a) Free will isn't something you're born with. It's something you develop -- in a very real sense, it's something you earn. It's precisely through reflection on what thoughts are yours and what come from outside that you develop the ability to assess your wants and decide which of them to follow, or even decide that even though you have no particular desire to do something, you're going to do it anyway.

5) You shed the material body, then the etheric double, then after a long period on the astral, the astral body. Your mental sheath and the undifferentiated seeds of the higher vehicles remain intact, and the patterns of consciousness you've picked up in the last life influence the bodies you have in the next. So there's some influence of body on body, but it's indirect.

replies.title: Re: An assorted set of questions
replies.content:
1) wow.

2) A necessary clarification, thank you. I did laugh at this one.

4) Oh so it’s the egregore that has the effect. Do banishings also help with egregores or once you tap into it the effects kick in and you have te rely on your self reflection?

4a) That is such a beautiful evolutionary design, and a great incentive for meditation!

5) And another, bigger incentive for meditation. What are undifferentiated seeds of the higher vehicles?

I really thought I had picked my questions at random from my notes.

replies.title: Re: An assorted set of questions
replies.content:
4) It depends on the banishing ritual. It won't do you much good to try to banish the Golden Dawn egregor using Golden Dawn banishing rituals, for example!

5) Okay, the very short form is that our souls start out at the very beginning on the seventh plane and descend plane by plane to the first or material plane, and from each plane they pass through, they pick up the capacity to build a body on that plane. Once they begin their incarnations, they start with a material body (while in mineral incarnations), then add an etheric body (while incarnated as plants), then add an astral body (while incarnated as animals), and so on. As humans we have a material body, an etheric body, an astral body, and a mental sheath, which is the first sketch of a mental body. (A few other animals, such as porpoises and whales, also have mental sheaths; most don't.) We will eventually have seven bodies, but the spiritual, causal, and divine bodies haven't even begun to develop yet. What we have are what I've called the undifferentiated seeds of the higher vehicles: structures of consciousness and subtle substance that will enable us, long ages from now, to evolve those bodies. The soul is the sum of those undifferentiated vehicles.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: An assorted set of questions
replies.content: We have a lot more work to do in it after death, because that's where our memories, passions, and habits of thought and feeling are stored, and all those have to be processed in the after-death state so that their essence can be absorbed by the soul.

replies.title: Re: An assorted set of questions
replies.content:
4a) There's a great edal of troublesome language and faulty assumptions plaguing the free will discussion that do a great deal to obfuscate the matter. Having spent years digging past all the trash to get to the core of the issue, and having become more familiar with the will after a great deal of meditation, I've come to much the same conclusion as our host.
Basically, to have free will means to be the originator of the intention to do A rather than B or C. You have free will to the degree that you practice self-determination.
Of course, complete self-determination is impossible for us. There are always outside factors which influence your thoughts and actions, but that fact by itself does not rob you of your free will.

comments.content:
May I ask a question partly to another reader?

I launched something during this Mercury Retrograde as it was better for my schedule and other projects not to put it off longer.

Was it Tunesmyth who maybe last year was wondering why an album hadn't been noticed much? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi anonymous,

Yeah, I’m not sure it’s fair to say that the Mercury Retrograde was at fault, so much as that the project— which ran into some snags along the way (its original release date wasn’t in the middle of Mercury Retrograde, for instance)— flowed naturally into a Mercury Retrograde release. I asked JMG about whether some sort of working to help connect my music to people to who would benefit from it might be unwise, but he said that was a fine kind of working to do. But ultimately I decided to simply entreaty Mercury on the matter through weekly Wednesday prayer (though this soon enough became daily planetary devotions without any particular requests for goodies). I believe all that I’ve reported here was a general statement that this seemed to be to positive effect. Specifically what had happened at the time I wrote that was not a bump in sales, but rather a culture organization in Detroit getting in touch with me and deciding to mount an orchestral program of my music, and even deciding to fly me in from Japan to be involved. Alas, more or less as soon as the tickets were purchased, coronavirus heated up to the point that all their projects were put on hiatus, indefinitely so far. So who knows how that will shake out in the end.

In the meantime, no, I haven’t relaunched the original album— although I do have the option to, as the record company (which only released as digital) graciously relinquished physical release rights back to us (seeing no money making potential in us). If I can manage sometime to raise the cash to self-finance a vinyl, I will do so— in which case, I may very well consult a professional astrologer about choosing a propitious date.

I don’t know if that’s what you were hoping to know about, but that’s where it stands for now!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
A question about possible harm to myself and others when practising the SoP, which I have been doing for the best part of a year now, and based on an incident in the past.
Back in the 70s, there was briefly interest in scientific investigation of the paranormal, after TV appearances by Uri Geller, and various books on the subject. I had a friend at the time who was strongly psychic - she was Irish as it happens and familiar with a lot of traditional mythology - and she persuaded a couple of us to volunteer for experiments being done by a team at a University where we were postgraduates. I was initially sceptical, but was supriised to find that I was the star pupil. I could make magnetised needles move in sealed jars by moving my hand or by tensing and relaxing the muscles of my abdomen , and could transmit patterns and words - but not receive them. The team leader said I was the only adult he had ever encountered who could do these things: all the other successful subjects had been children.
But it wasn't all positive, and I began to destroy things. Once, when I entered the laboratory, a light-bulb in a lamp used to film the experiments exploded in pieces; Several times, electrical equipment failed to work, and I even became physically ill during the experiments. Twice, I returned home and blew the master fuse just by touching one switch. The second time, the electrician who came said he had never seen anything like it in his life. I gave up the experiments after that.
I've had some weird experiences with electronic equipment on and off throughout my life, but nothing that serious or destructive. I have practised energy medicine on myself (and others) for decades, and I'm finely attuned to movements of energy in the body. In the days when I had acupuncture, I could usually tell the therapist what the effect of the needles was on the flow of energy in my body.
Now then, you've often said that magicians are made, not born, and I assume this is true more widely: people are not born with the kind of capabilities I was tested for. They were something I picked up at the time, and have been disappearing slowly since. At least I hope so. Does the manipulation of energy in theSoP put me or others in any danger, given my experiences of fifty years ago? So far, I haven't had any abreaction, but I'd like to be reassured.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, quite the contrary; you should be fine. The sort of thing you did in the 1970s can be inborn -- it has to do with certain states of the etheric body. The SoP works on the next level up, the astral level, and so tends to bring the bodies further down (etheric and material) into balance.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I am reading the great essay at the beginning of The Cosmic Shape. There, the descriptions of the English/Celtic deities is similar but different from the descriptions of them in the DMH, and I think I see why from reading that essay. If I understand right, the collective unconscious contains the archetypes, which are deep-rooted forces of consciousness and/or patterns of behavior that are universal, and myths make these archetypes understandable and concrete to people by personifying them. The act of personification is 'constructed' through time based on observation of natural patterns and human behavior, and this act of construction doesn't make the myths any less true because they use local circumstance to explain aspects of the collective unconscious.

In the discussion of Virgil's poem, he writes that the Roman emperor encouraged him to write the poem in order to rebuild the connection between humans and nature because this connection had been lost - resulting in soil depletion. Nichols also writes about how to do the same for todays's time and for the same purpose. I was a little off-put by this at first, as it seems similar to how advertising works to spread the myth of consumerism, and mass thaumaturgy, rather than something which seeks primarily to help individuals to connect to nature.

It's similar to the balance hinted at in the opening quote to this week's Magic Monday: between the skeptic and the occultist who believes everything. Perhaps I was believing too much in the literal description of the deities as they were presented in the DMH, and now that the 'trick' has been revealed, I find myself still a little confused. Any insight you may offer would be welcome!

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nichols realized later on that he had to work with individuals first and foremost -- that's why he became a Druid, and founded the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids. The "Virgil option" didn't work -- he was enough of a classicist to know that it didn't work for Rome, and a keen enough observer of his time that he could tell that it wasn't working for Britain.

As for the deities, you now have two mythic personifications to work with. Use meditation, ritual, and imaginal experience to work with those and find your own set of mythic personifications. That's one of the things that all this is about!

comments.title: Mating on the Seven Planes
comments.content:
Good morning,

I'm reading Dion Fortune's 'The Esoteric Philosophy of Love and Marriage'. In most relationship advice you find in books or on youtube, the emphasis is on making things work with the partner you find yourself with: clear communication, honesty and vulnerability, looking closely at which values you hold in common, etc.

Fortune instead seems to place a great emphasis on finding (or being cosmically led to) a partner that can fulfill one's needs on each of the planes of being on which one is currently operating. There's something appealing in this, as it seems to explain why I've had trouble finding partners I really feel connected to (I'm a thirty-year-old man seeking a female partner).

On the other hand, Fortune's view also holds out a temptation to elitism to which I think I'm susceptible... How do you assess this book of hers, and the advice she gives of waiting until the right match arrives?

Thank you,
Dylan

replies.title: Re: Mating on the Seven Planes
replies.content:
In many ways, that's the least satisfactory of Fortune's books -- and it's worth noting that her own marriage was a disaster, and ended in adultery and divorce. This should suggest that her advice may not be all that sound!

Maybe one person in a million will be able to find a partner that satisfies them on all the planes of manifestation. Most of us have to make do, at best, with one or two planes of compatibility, and of these the astral -- the plane of emotion -- is the most important in the long run. Of course the satisfaction has to go both ways! So staking all on the hope of finding a perfect match may not be a good idea -- what if she satisfies all your needs, but you don't satisfy all hers?

replies.title: Re: Mating on the Seven Planes
replies.content:
Thank you for a wonderfully clear answer. It is such a relief to hear a plain assessment after trying to muddle through the questions myself.

You wrote a few Mondays back about cancer being associated with personalities too focused on purity, and this seems to have been the proximate cause of Fortune's death. It occurs to me that when I've noted her views on sexuality diverging from yours, the topic often has to do with questions of purity or abstinence.

Of course a number of other questions related to my own search flood to mind, but I know your time is limited!

I'll stick with these: What does that astral or emotional compatibility that you mentioned look like? And do you have any alternative esoteric resources on love and sexuality to suggest?

replies.title: Re: Mating on the Seven Planes
replies.content:
Well, my experience is that it shows up first as a sense of mutual comfortableness. My wife and I met through mutual friends, remained on the periphery of each other's worlds for about six months, then moved into the same student household. Within a day or so we were talking as though we'd known each other for years; we tumbled into bed shortly after that, and within a few days our housemates were joking about how we were acting like an old married couple -- hanging out together on the couch, cuddling or just sitting near each other reading or doing homework. Things proceeded from there; we'll be celebrating our 36th anniversary later this month.

As for books on the subject, I wish I had some to recommend.

replies.title: Re: Mating on the Seven Planes
replies.content:
I'm also reading this book and find the esoteric philosophy useful, but haven't gotten far enough to comment on the rest. Luckily, I happen to have one of those great marriages that seems to work really well through thick or thin. And we've been through some hella thin times together...

You'll know when you find her. Wishing you the best.

comments.title: Star Ships by Gordon White
comments.content:
JMG,

Have you read Star Ships by Gordon White? I'm reading it directly on the heels of your Secret of the Temple, I can't help but think you'd be interested. On page 35, while discussing the stone-age temple complex Gobekli Tepe in what is now Southeast Turkey, he writes that the central pillars of each chamber hum audibly when struck with the palm of the hand, and were originally constructed to emphasize this effect. The surrounding chambers - which include a ring of stone pilllars - possibly acted as ampitheatres of sorts.

He then writes on page 36:

"The sonic qualities of Neolithic temples have been gaining wider awareness these past few years. The bluestones of Stonehenge "sing" when hit with rocks. In Western European tombs and mounds resonances tend to cluster around the 110-122 Hertz rage, the same as a baritone voice. Maltese temples - currently considered Neolithic though very likely Paleolithic in origin - provode similar results.Like astronomy, the scientific understanding of sound has typically fallen outside the more humanities-focused interpretations of ancient history. The implications of these Paleolithic capacities are astounding, and cry out to be explored, both personally and academically."

He then goes on to make a case that Gobekli Tepe was a center of cultural and technological exchange, and symbolically compares one of its features to a sword in a stone. Gobekli Tepe is pre-agricultural, but he also suggests that it might have played a significant role in the transition to agriculture, and traces its potential influence to Egypt and Sumeria.

Now I'm only 45 pages in, but found the potential connection to the megalithic end of the material studied in your book tantalizing. Star Ships covers a lot of ground, and I don't know how much more of relevance there may or may not be in the rest of the book. Thought you might be interested, though.

In general I get the sense that the threads you and he are following cross fairly often. Incidentally, I'm also following a thread which overlaps both of yours. Mine seems like it might be being inspired by some as yet unkown force or entity. Like whatever I'm trying to articulate is itself trying to be articulated. Did you get that sense while writing The Secret of the Temple or The Mysteries of Merlin?

Thanks!

comments.title: Honey Jar Success
comments.content:
My wife and I typically spend a week on the Outer Banks of North Carolina with my parents and sister every year. Since my daughter was born last June my relationship with my parents (to a degree) and especially with my sister have become very strained. My parents and sister have taken every decision my wife and I make in raising our daughter as if it is a personal condemnation of their lifestyle. It's incredibly frustrating and it meant that our Christmas together was tense and aggravating. This coupled with the fact that they are devout members of the Blue Church made me very nervous to spend a week with them. However, it's also important to me that we maintain our relationship and I have hopes that we can learn to communicate more effectively.

In anticipation of the trip, I assembled a honey jar on Friday, May 22nd (the new moon) on the hour of Venus. A few times a week for the month leading up to the trip I charged the honey jar when I was doing my LIRP. When we arrived at the beach cottage last Sunday I buried the jar under the cottage where we would be staying together. I was absolutely amazed by the results! I had the best, most open conversations with my family that I have ever had. Generally, we were all patient with one another and it felt like we made a lot of progress in learning how to communicate together more effectively.

Should I follow this same formula every time I will be spending time with them (usually four times a year - mostly on holidays)? Is there any way I can improve this or are there any other actions that I should be taking? I already have a consistent meditation, divination, and LBRP/Middle Pillar practice and am journaling to get to the bottom of a lot of the issues I have with my family. Thank you so much for your help!

replies.title: Re: Honey Jar Success
replies.content: You've got something that works, so keep doing it! Yes, you should do it every single time. A successful spell like this lays down what Dion Fortune calls "a track in space" (that's spelled "morphogenetic field" by Rupert Sheldrake) which makes future repetitions even more likely to succeed.

comments.title: The Moon and Mental Health
comments.content:
I have been struggling with my mental health for the past six months. I had a major panic attack walking into the office one day in January, complete with the embarrassment of a 911 call on my behalf. During my subsequent two weeks off, I started researching causes for my anxiety and depression beyond just overwork at an architecture firm.

I had always noticed over my young lifetime of struggles with anxiety that the moon seemed to have some effect. I repeatedly found myself anxious out of nowhere, and would look at the night sky only to have the full moon shining back at me.

During my research into this topic, I stumbled across a recent article talking about certain medical professionals who are starting to admit there is a connection for some people. https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20190731-is-the-moon-impacting-your-mood-and-wellbeing This is one of those situations you’re fond of mentioning where if modern science can’t explain it, it throws up its hands. Quite distressing for me, in this case. My moods tended to be worst around the time of the new and full phases. This past weekend was particularly bad, starting on Wednesday evening.

I noticed after I started taking antidepressants that the moon connection became subdued, but recently I have noticed it becoming more intensified again, not just for me but also my wife (who has been on medication for years). Being isolated together can account for part of it, but I wonder if there are any occult connections for this sort of thing. Or gentle solutions. I seem to remember you mentioning in the past that anyone with mental health problems should steer clear of magic, so in my own Druid practice I have steered clear of anything beyond color breathing as a part of meditation. Any thoughts on this scientific dead-end welcome, and I will continue to check my Farmers Almanac only after the “black dog days” so I don’t taint the data with foreknowledge!

-Architrains

replies.title: Re: The Moon and Mental Health
replies.content: The connection between the moon and mental states is well attested -- there's a reason people used to speak of "lunacy," after all. When I worked in nursing homes, everyone dreaded the night of the full moon, because all the dementia patients would be hyperactive; at the new moon, they'd be sleepy and easily handled. What exactly to do about it, though, is a complex question. I wonder, though, if planetary charity directed to the Moon might help.

replies.title: Re: various questions
replies.content:
1. These types of trivial synchronicities have been happening to me for almost a year, and are occurring almost daily now.

One explanation from a rational/scientific perspective is that due to various types of cognitive bias, as soon as one starts to pay attention to synchronicity, one will notice the phenomenon more frequently.

However, I don't think that tells the whole story. Evolutionary emergence, increasing complexity, and radical shifts in consciousness (both personal and collective) may also account for it. As discussed by Richard Tarnas in "Cosmos and Psyche," Jung believed that experiences of synchronicity, like dreams, could move the psyche toward greater wholeness and integration. Jung considered the underlying meaning that linked synchronistic inner and outer events to be archetypal in nature. But you have to tease out the meanings for yourself.

2. Maybe you could ask the spirit of your bonsai tree how it feels about it, and thank it for bringing pleasure and beauty into your life.

I do my SOP daily at near the border of my back yard and the tall grass meadow just beyond it. We have a compost pile in this border area as well, just inside the tall grass, about 40-50 feet from where I perform my ritual and then sit for meditation.
Last week, I strolled out to my usual spot and began my SOP in the usual fashion, facing East, which happens to be the direction of the compost pile. I went through the elemental cross and the ritual for the air element looking East, and then turned and performed the fire element to the South. When I turned West, before I could complete tracing my blue triangle, I was startled by a loud rustling/snorting/grunting sound, and I wheeled quickly around to see a large black bear (much taller than the tall grass) bolting from the compost pile, heading into the nearby woods. I was frozen to the spot for a few seconds as my brain processed the scene and watched the bear disappear.
I took a few deep breaths and then continued on to finish my ritual successfully - so JMG, if anyone ever asks how to proceed if their SOP is interrupted by a bear, I can attest that just picking up where you left off works :-)
So, I realized that the bear would have had to have been there by the compost pile the whole time. I was looking pretty much right at the area around the compost pile the whole time I was facing East - probably around a solid 5 minutes or so, and I saw nothing (I do the ritual with my eyes open, although I suppose one explanation is that I wasn't really "seeing" what was in front of me because I was engaged in the visualization required by the ritual). That possible explanation aside, it really made me think about how you've said that there are techniques of magical training that can make one seem invisible by willing that the person remain unseen.

1) Do you think that it is possible that this type of "working" could be instinctual for some higher level animals, such as a bear? It also struck me as an explanation for the Bigfoot phenomenon - perhaps the species has a highly developed instinctual skill for this? After this experience with not seeing the bear when it should have been in plain site, it certainly has made me consider the credibility of Bigfoot actually existing.

2) I've noticed that the woods and meadows around my rural homestead seem particularly vibrant and filled with vitality this summer. I'm not sure if it's because it's the first summer I've been doing magical training, or if there is something bigger going on, but there seems to be more wildlife and more lush and varied plant life than normal. Entering into the woods feels like crossing a threshold into a magical realm. I haven't felt this since I was a child (36 now). Have you noticed any signs of increased vitality in Nature this summer JMG? Anyone else?

Thanks so much for Magic Monday's, your astrological forecasts, and all of your writings. They've brought so much value to me spiritually and intellectually.

comments.title: Magic bath follow up
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Several months ago I asked a question about how to respond to the suspicion that my family and my business are the focus of negative magic from a group of people. You helpfully recommended the hoodoo bath, red protection amulet, and Dion Fortune's book Psychic Self Defense. I followed your advice and here's what happened:

Immediately after the very first hoodoo bath, I had an intense full body burning sensation that lasted all day, along with mild euphoria and feeling quite energetic, positive, and full of life. You commented that perhaps my pepper was just extra spicy, but I can definitively say that isn't the case, as I've been using the same bag of habanero since then and have never had anything like the same experience. I still do hoodoo baths almost daily and I think they are helping me stay clear and less affected by other people's nastiness.

I'm on my second amulet and so far so good.

I found Dion Fortune's book to be refreshingly sensible. I especially liked her framework of first ruling out a possible physical cause, then ruling out a possible psychological cause, before considering a possible occult cause. The book led me to conclude that I had indeed been the focus of hostile magic, and that I had spent too long trying to push through the effects without drawing on outside input because I didn't want to sound crazy.

In general since taking your advice I feel less anxious, more focused, and more able to make headway in the world. Some longstanding issues in business and personal life that I attribute to negative magic have eased considerably, although moreso on the business side. Negative trends have turned slightly positive, and there are more good things developing that hopefully will allow me to say things have really turned around by the end of the year.

I have some follow up questions:

1. I made an amulet for a friend of mine who is quite sensitive and often overwhelmed. She said the amulet made her feel strange and unwell, and after a day she refused to wear it any further, or even discuss why. To me it seemed slightly sinister that she would immediately reject a protective amulet. What do you make of this?

2. My wife is still acting in a way that to me is compulsively negative, very fear based, and destructive to our family. She won't get any help, and also won't wear the protective amulet, although she does put it under her pillow at night. She has been very into reading about the recent BLM movement/riots on social media and that's making her more emotionally deranged, reactive, and borderline panicked. I have theories about what is driving her behavior, namely a family history of lies and manipulation, combined with depletion from childbirth, and possible demonic influence. She is acting as if compelled against all sense to undermine her life, happiness, and relationships, but I'm not really sure what I can do about it. Do you recommend anything that a not-very magically adept person like myself can do to help someone who seems distressed on multiple psychological and spiritual levels?

I realize that this may be too vague and/or too big a question, but addressing negative magic attack seems to have helped alter previously static negative patterns in my life, and this is the remaining big issue that I struggle to make sense of.

Thanks for all your work, and for the many interesting and thought provoking commentors on your various sites.

replies.title: Re: Magic bath follow up
replies.content:
1) One of the unwelcome discoveries you make as a mage is that a lot of people are in love with their problems and will do almost anything to avoid giving them up.

2) There's very little you can do. Are you comfortable praying to a deity? That would be one potentially useful option.

replies.content:
1. That unfortunately sounds about right.

2. Thanks for this, I will take up prayer in a more serious way. I was raised in a bland Christian family so I feel like I have a basic relationship with (as my latino Catholic friends fondly call him) the Big J.

replies.content: You might consider also asking for the intercessions of the Mother of God-- she is known to look after women and children, and is particularly called on for help in anything childbirth-related.

comments.title: Drugs & the Occult
comments.content:
My enquiry is somewhat connected to your recent “power of the mind” post on your other blog – but because it also touches on individuals who are not mentioned in that blog, I figured it might be appropriate to ask it here.

In that post you mentioned that Mary Baker Eddy was addicted to morphine later in life. As soon as I read that, I remembered that Helena Blavatsky was a chain-smoker. And I’m sure that if I was to do some digging, I would find a fair number of occultists who were either habitual users of, or addicted to, one or more powerful drugs. Now, it just so happens that I follow a mystical path that prohibits the consumption of any addictive substance on a daily basis other than moderate consumption of caffeine (unless stronger drugs are required for medical purposes); at the same time, I try not to judge those who do not follow my “squeaky clean” path. And I do recall that you have made some not-so-favourable comments regarding magic and the use of psychedelics/alcohol.

So, I am trying to “make peace” with the fact that there are occultists whom I admire yet who were engaged in habits that if not harmful at the spiritual level, certainly are detrimental at the physical, etheric and possibly also the astral levels. Would not occultists of a high order be aware, at least intuitively, of this form of self-harm and therefore avoid it? I am not losing sleep on this issue, but would like to know your view on this matter.

Thanks, as always, for sharing your vast occult knowledge!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Drugs & the Occult
replies.content: Occultists are human beings like the rest of us, and some of them end up with self-destructive habits of one kind or another. It's also possible to use some drugs recreationally without any particular trouble. Some paths restrict such things, others don't, and I suspect the founders of those paths knew what they were doing in both cases and set limits accordingly.

comments.title: On Wicca & the Goddess
comments.content:
Last week you - or was it you? - mentioned the Wiccan goddess as "being cobbled together from its & pieces." A day later I had a dream of being one of three maintenance, or possibly constitutions workers, trying to sculpt a sacred image out of Styrofoam with our utility knives and pocket knives. In a city where sacred images abounded, bright with color, lively in lifelikeness, and moderately-to-genius-level well crafted. As I was waking, an inner voice said "wrong tools, wrong medium, and - wrong job!"

Which gave me a bad week of head-scratching. Superficially, it meant I should go haring off after the dazzling array of pantheons around, but experience told me that's always proven a bad idea whenever I did. I always came back what I'd always done and had.

And my contact with Her seemed a but, thin, so I was Told to reread the Charge of the Goddess. Well, Doreen Valiente's masterpiece, the basic statement of the Wiccan faith, is just as clearly as patchwork, with the seams showing as clearly as those on my crazy quilt(where it's deliberate.) It even includes a terribly clunky piece of grammar from trying to force what had been third-person (Aradia speaking for and of Diana) into first person. Not to mention the cognitive jump from "party down!" to "Keep pure your highest ideals."

Yet, the bones of the faith are solid. That the center seems weak and for me, always has, is because back home, the actual workings were taught in the various groups, none of which I - and my fellow collection of misfits - ever found entry to. Then, too, the public rituals were put on by an umbrella organization, heavily into both Golden Dawn and New Thought, and the content seemed to weaken the further and further we were from the teachings of the founder of the local pagan community (who did know her stiff; she was before my time)and those directly trained by her.

The one who trained us asked me just last year if she was Wiccan and how you defined it. I mentioned those basics and she said she guessed then that she was. She's a ceremonial magician and astrologer, and taught us 101 out of Silver Ravenwolf, hoping to follow up with heavy-duty Golden Dawn. Which she did, and 90% of it went way over my head. The one who picked up most of us - the least functional! - has a genius for bringing out the best in everyone, but is more or less out of the Fairy tradition, mostly less - basically, if you ask me, an animist - and whose magic work ranges from pop-slogan therapy to kindergarten level to powerful.

So - working solitary with worship, meditation,and divination has been very productive personally, and yes, I did meditate and got "define wisdom." I did ask the oracle where to go from here and got The Cat, The Book, and The Rose." So if I find out the mystery the book symbolizes (and yes, my faith does tell me where to find that mystery, and it's not from any outside source) I'll come out of it a very healthy woman. However - I've been impelled to run the matter by you and hunt down - yes, more books. Any advice?

The Grey Badger

replies.title: Re: On Wicca & the Goddess
replies.content: Yes, it was me. There was a lot of goddess syncretism in early 20th century Britain -- the notion that all goddesses are one Goddess was all over the avant-garde, and gave us Robert Graves' The White Goddess and a great many other artifacts, including Wicca. That said, if it works for you, it works for you. I'm not at all sure what further advice I can offer, because I've never been attracted to any form of witchcraft and so know very little about it.

comments.title: Overcoming bad mental habits
comments.content:
JMG, I have Asperger’s syndrome, and I would call it severe based on just how badly I misunderstand people, and how I elicit bad feelings from others.

I’m in crisis now, with a very delayed process of divorce from a 20-year marriage. But the worst crisis has to do with my older (16) daughter who is very anxious around me, and has basically rejected me. (My 10 year-old daughter and I have a much better relationship). By way of background, all members of the family are in counseling, including me.

At this point, I am out of the house, sleeping in my office, though I use the house for a base camp, for showering, laundry, eating a few meals and such. I still do yardwork, take my younger daughter on outings, and even my older daughter opted to go alone with me a few days ago for a 90 minute drive in the country; I’ll be bringing our spare car into the garage soon so my wife can use it safely after the divorce (she is relearning to drive after 25 years). My wife and I are on speaking terms, but it is strained.

One thing I’m aware of is that my relationship with my older daughter seems to recapitulate my dad’s relationship with me; I’ve known this for a long time, but I did not realize until recently just how badly it impacted her. I now have a real fear of loosing her forever. I recall you once mentioned how you found Epictetus’ writings (esp. the Enchiridion) in helping you through the loss of your son, so I took my copy with me to my office-home, and chapter 43 immediately offered itself: “Every circumstance comes with two handles, with one of which you can hold it, while with other conditions are unsupportable. If you brother mistreats you, don’t try to come to grips with it by by dwelling on the wrong that he’s done (because that approach makes it unbearable); remind yourself that he’s your brother, that you grew up together; the you’ll find that you can bear it”.

When I read that, I was startled how it succinctly pointed out my father’s approach to me, and my approach to too many other people, including my daughter. Yes there is “Asperger’s”, but there is also something else I can control. So at this point, I send my daughter 2 texts per day, one in the morning, one in the evening. Everything I send is positive, and simply intended to elicit a warm feeling, even a simple “Good night. I love you. Dad”; I don’t ask anything of her, or try to persuade her anything. She has so far responded to two of them, first writing ‘yeah thanks’, then the next day ‘Thank you’.

I am full on with daily LRP and MPE rituals. I set up an alter for twice daily prayers and offerings to the 7 deities with planetary attributions, which thankfully includes Mercury/Hermes, my patron god. For the alter covering, I use a cotton pillow-case that was exquisitely embroidered by my grandmother 70 years ago. I use 2 votive candles in simple glass candle holders, incense, and an offering plate (with a cup for wine for the evening ritual). For the moment, I don’t have statues, though one for Hermes is on order, and I rely on printed images of statues for the time being. I use the Orphic Hymns (you helpfully reproduced them in The Art and Practice of Geomancy), followed by personal prayer to the god/goddess, followed by a period of listening.

I am revving up to renew my daily meditation and divination practices.

Do you have any other suggestions on how I might turn around this devastating negative mental stance of mine? It’s now or never for me.

replies.title: Re: Overcoming bad mental habits
replies.content:
Ouch. That's got to be very difficult for you; please accept my sympathy. My father, who also has Aspergers syndrome, had a very rough time with his divorce.

The practices you've described, plus counseling, plus Epictetus, are what I'd have recommended. Beyond that, if you can handle getting professional massage, that can be a huge help in dealing with the tremendous stress you're under; I don't really know anything else to suggest.

replies.title: Re: a few magical questions from a beginner

comments.title: Spirit possession in children
comments.content:
Dear Michael,
I greatly appreciate your posts and insights on this (for me) new arena. I was raised UCC Protestant, and must say, Christianity always seemed like a great idea, but never felt quite natural/right to me. I am sensitive at some level to the spirit world, and have been surprised by some impromptu conversations and harmless interactions with several that have passed. I have been, recently, much relieved to read your posts. I was immediately comfortable at a gut level with a lot of what you convey, especially in regards to re-incarnation.

My question is, are young children (age 3 and under) susceptible to spirit possession? My entire life (now age 55) I have accommodated a second persona (if that’s the right word for it). For the most part we get along, though at times not so much. Though I am female, the second persona is either male or perhaps non-sexual. My earliest recollections at about 3 years of age are of battling this incoming force quite determinedly and for quite some time. By 1st grade we were accommodating each other and most of my childhood and rest of my life I have spent dealing with persona #2 as best I can.

Question #2; is there any way to rid myself of this persona, or at my age is it better to simply continue with what I am used to? Persona #2 is derogatory, demeaning, and generally negative. I am the polar opposite, possibly by necessity. Perhaps I am simply nuts? Sigh. A question I have asked myself all of my life. Anyway, I would love to hear more about your thoughts on this type of situation. I did purchase two of your books and look forward to learning more through them, as well.

replies.title: Re: Spirit possession in children
replies.content: Yes, young children are susceptible to spirit possession; that's why it's a bad idea to practice major magical ritual around them.  As for what you can do about it, it depends on fine details that I may not be able to judge, and also on how deep you want to get into ceremonial magic. It's possible at a certain level of skill to bind such a being and expel it from the psyche, though that takes time and hard work; some people find that regular practice of banishing rituals will take care of it.

replies.content: Could the second persona be the OP's shadow self, and not a separate entity?

comments.title: Anger and the planes.
comments.content:
Archdruid,

I've spent the last week meditating upon the way emotions and events manifest upon the planes. Anything we experience manifests on several planes, so do our reactions to those experiences.

The reactions and experiences have different impacts on different planes, and can be dealt with differently on each plane. "every reaction has an equal and opposite reaction, but only upon the plane where it manifests." Meaning that the physical law mentioned about isn't the same on every plane.

For example. Anger manifests most heavily on the etheric and astral planes. It is easier to deal with in the realm of astral or the mind, because when you push it up a plane you cause it multiply (that is break into it's component parts, that can then be dealt with individually.) Anger becomes the event and our reaction.

In imagination, or the astral plane, we can create a target for our anger or channel it and transform it into something else.

In mind we can dismantle the anger, and analyze it for meaning.

In the physical we can channel the anger into force. Martial arts teaches us to take the heat of fire and transform it into a disciplined cold fire, purposeful and measured.

Each experience and reaction alter all the planes, and we change accordingly. Those reactions build up and can be transposed to meet new experiences. A warrior who learns to fight, learns to paint, learns to speak,and learns to garden will inherently be a more dangerous that the warrior who only learns to fight. The former has a multiplicity of potential built up within themselves.

Regards,

Syfen

comments.title: friend in need
comments.content:
Emergency last-hour long-shot question here.

There is a mated pair of swans who visit the stretch of estuary I live on nearly daily. Each year they raise their brood of cygnets and my wife and I watch them grow up through the year. It's not unusual for a cygnet to disappear between one day and the next. Natural hazards from snapping turtles to coyotes take their due. We're used to this.

Today, though, I discovered that one of this year's four cygnets has a large fishing lure hooked into one foot. The cygnet isn't in any obvious immediate distress, but if the lure stays in place he or she is doomed to a slow death that might take weeks or months. It's not possible to intervene physically. (The birds are not tame and I have no equipment or expertise to attempt a capture-rescue. I have to confess I spent a good part of the afternoon in the river, hoping that by some miracle of sympathy they would come close enough for me to assist, but no such miracle occurred. Yes, I can try again in coming days.) The relevant wildlife authorities won't intervene.

Am I correct that my only realistic magical option is prayer? I'm praying for the cygnet's well-being, leaving it up to the Power of the Restoring Tide to decide whether that's best achieved with a turn of fortune (in theory the hook could come detached somehow), a fast passing, or some other development (such as a chance for me to rescue after all as the situation evolves). I'd certainly try anything by way of natural magic that might help. My limited ritual skills (SoP) don't seem applicable, and mental magic helps me keep perspective but can't apply much beyond that. Any ideas?

replies.title: Re: friend in need
replies.content: As far as I know, yes, prayer is the only option you've got.
